skill_name: Quantifiers: Advanced (A little vs Little / Neither/Either/Each / Hardly any)

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: There is ___ milk left in the fridge — we need to buy more today.
distractor_rationale: a little means some, which contradicts the need to buy more immediately
a few is used with countable nouns; milk is uncountable
few is used with countable nouns; milk is uncountable

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: We have ___ time before the bus leaves, so let's get a coffee quickly.
distractor_rationale: little means almost none, which contradicts the suggestion to get a coffee
a few is used with countable nouns; time is uncountable
few is used with countable nouns; time is uncountable

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: ___ student in the class has to submit a different project topic by Friday.
distractor_rationale: every is grammatically possible but the sentence stresses individual assignment of distinct topics, which each expresses more precisely; however, the key distractor error tested here is either/neither — note: 'every' is accepted as near-equivalent, so this item is adjusted — see rationale note
either refers to one of two options only, not to all members of a group
neither expresses a negative meaning about two things, which contradicts the positive requirement to submit

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: The doctor said there is ___ chance of a full recovery, so the family feels hopeful.
distractor_rationale: little means almost no chance, which contradicts the family feeling hopeful
a few is used with countable nouns; chance used as an uncountable mass noun here requires a little
few is used with countable nouns; chance as an uncountable noun requires a little

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: ___ of the two candidates has the right experience for this job, so we will advertise again.
distractor_rationale: either means one or the other is acceptable, which contradicts the decision to advertise again
each is used to refer to every individual in a group separately, not to express that both fail a condition
none is used with groups of three or more, not specifically with two candidates

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: There were ___ seats left when we arrived at the theatre, so we had to stand at the back.
distractor_rationale: a few means some, which contradicts having to stand at the back because of the shortage
a little is used with uncountable nouns; seats is countable, and the meaning of some also contradicts standing
not some is not a standard English quantifier phrase; the correct negative form would be not any, but hardly any better expresses almost none

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: ___ side of the road has a pavement, so cyclists must be very careful when riding here.
distractor_rationale: neither means not one and not the other, but the sentence says cyclists must be careful because pavements exist on both sides — wait, re-reading: 'either side has a pavement' means both sides have one, which is why cyclists must be careful sharing the road — this is the correct positive both-sides reading of either
each is used to refer to individual members one by one, not to express that both sides share the same feature simultaneously
every is used with groups of three or more and does not collocate naturally with side of the road in this two-item context

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: The report showed that ___ of the students had completed the reading before class, which surprised the teacher.
distractor_rationale: a little is used with uncountable nouns; students is countable, and a little cannot quantify people

few is grammatically possible with a countable noun but cannot be followed by of the students in standard usage without the article; more importantly, hardly any more precisely conveys almost none and is the target form
neither is used exclusively with two items and cannot refer to a whole class of students

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: Although the project is nearly finished, there is still ___ work to do before we can present it to the client.
distractor_rationale: little means almost no work remains, which contradicts the clause saying there is still work to do before presenting
a few is used with countable nouns; work here is an uncountable noun
hardly any means almost none, which contradicts the implication that some remaining work must be done before the presentation

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: ___ member of the research team is responsible for writing up their own section of the final report.
distractor_rationale: either refers to one of exactly two options and cannot distribute individual responsibility across a whole team
neither expresses that not one of two people does something, which contradicts the positive assignment of responsibility
all requires a plural noun; member is singular, so all member is ungrammatical without the plural members, and the sentence specifically tests each with a singular noun

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: ___ student in the class has to submit a separate report by Friday.
distractor_rationale: either refers to one of two options and cannot distribute a single task across a whole class
neither expresses a negative meaning for two things, which contradicts the requirement to submit a report
every one requires 'of' before a noun phrase and cannot directly precede a singular noun without it

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: We have ___ a little time before the meeting starts, so let's get a coffee quickly.
distractor_rationale: little means almost no time, which contradicts the suggestion to get a coffee
a few is used with countable nouns; time is uncountable
few is used with countable nouns; time is uncountable

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: ___ of the two job candidates was suitable for the position, so the manager decided to advertise again.
distractor_rationale: either of the two would mean one of them was suitable, which contradicts the decision to advertise again
each is used to refer to members of a group individually and does not express the negative meaning required here
both means the two candidates were suitable together, which contradicts the decision to advertise again

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: There were ___ any seats left on the train by the time Tom arrived at the station, so he had to stand for the whole journey.
distractor_rationale: not any means there were zero seats, but the sentence implies Tom did board the train and stand, suggesting a tiny number existed; also 'not any' does not collocate with 'any' in this pattern without restructuring the sentence
a little does not collocate with the countable plural noun 'seats' and does not fit the 'any' structure
scarcely enough changes the grammatical structure of the sentence and does not fit the fixed 'hardly any' pattern required before 'seats'

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: There is ___ milk left in the fridge, so we need to buy more today.
distractor_rationale: 'a little' means some milk remains and implies no problem, contradicting 'we need to buy more'
'a few' is used with countable nouns, but 'milk' is uncountable
'few' is used with countable nouns, but 'milk' is uncountable

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: We have ___ time before the bus arrives, so let's get a coffee.
distractor_rationale: 'little' means almost none and would contradict the suggestion to get coffee, which requires enough time
'a few' is used with countable nouns, but 'time' is uncountable
'few' is used with countable nouns, but 'time' is uncountable

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: There are ___ seats available for tonight's concert, so you should book one now.
distractor_rationale: 'a few' means some seats are available and implies no urgency, contradicting 'you should book one now'
'little' is used with uncountable nouns, but 'seats' is countable and plural
'a little' is used with uncountable nouns, but 'seats' is countable and plural

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: ___ student in the class has to submit their assignment by Friday without exception.
distractor_rationale: 'Either' refers to one of two options and cannot distribute a requirement across an entire class
'Neither' is negative and means not one of two, making the sentence contradict the requirement
'Every one' requires 'of the students' to be grammatical in this sentence position

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: ___ of the two job candidates was suitable, so the manager decided to advertise the position again.
distractor_rationale: 'Either' means one or the other would be acceptable, contradicting the decision to readvertise
'Each' distributes positively across items and cannot express that none was suitable
'Both' means the two together, implying both were suitable, which contradicts the context

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: The doctor said I can take ___ tablet in the morning or at night — it doesn't matter which.
distractor_rationale: 'neither' means not one of the two, which would mean I cannot take the tablet at all, contradicting the doctor's instruction
'each' distributes across all items in a group and does not express a choice between two alternatives
'every' requires a plural or collective context and does not express a binary choice between two options

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: There are ___ students who still haven't returned their library books, which is a big problem for the school.
distractor_rationale: 'few' would mean almost none haven't returned books, which contradicts 'a big problem for the school'
'a little' is used with uncountable nouns, but 'students' is countable and plural
'little' is used with uncountable nouns, but 'students' is countable and plural

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: ___ of the employees had heard about the new policy before the meeting, which surprised the director.
distractor_rationale: 'Not any' is an absolute negative meaning zero employees knew, whereas 'hardly any' means almost none, which is the nuanced meaning supported by the surprise expressed
'A little' is used with uncountable nouns and cannot modify the countable noun 'employees'

'Few of' requires a determiner before the noun phrase: 'Few of the employees' is grammatical, but this distractor is listed without 'the', making it structurally incomplete as presented, and more importantly it lacks the emphatic near-zero nuance that 'hardly any' carries in this context

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: ___ team in the competition gets exactly the same amount of preparation time to ensure the contest is fair.
distractor_rationale: 'Either' refers to one of only two options, but the competition has more than two teams, making it semantically wrong
'Neither' is negative and implies no team gets preparation time, directly contradicting the sentence meaning
'Any' does not carry the distributive meaning of 'each one individually' and would not convey that every single team gets the same time

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: There is ___ evidence to support the claim, so the report cannot be published in its current form.
distractor_rationale: 'a little' means some evidence exists and would not justify blocking publication, contradicting the consequence stated
'hardly any of' requires a specific noun phrase with a determiner after 'of', making it grammatically incomplete before the bare noun 'evidence'
'few' is used with countable nouns, but 'evidence' is uncountable

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: There is a little milk left in the fridge, so we need to buy more today.
content.error_sentence: There is a little milk left in the fridge, so we need to buy more today.
content.corrected_sentence: There is little milk left in the fridge, so we need to buy more today.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: Each of the students have to submit their assignment before Friday.
content.error_sentence: Each of the students have to submit their assignment before Friday.
content.corrected_sentence: Each of the students has to submit their assignment before Friday.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: Neither of the two candidates were qualified for the position, so the manager decided to advertise again.
content.error_sentence: Neither of the two candidates were qualified for the position, so the manager decided to advertise again.
content.corrected_sentence: Neither of the two candidates was qualified for the position, so the manager decided to advertise again.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: We had little time before the train left, so we quickly grabbed something to eat at the station.
content.error_sentence: We had little time before the train left, so we quickly grabbed something to eat at the station.
content.corrected_sentence: We had a little time before the train left, so we quickly grabbed something to eat at the station.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: Either side of the road were covered in fallen leaves after the storm last night.
content.error_sentence: Either side of the road were covered in fallen leaves after the storm last night.
content.corrected_sentence: Either side of the road was covered in fallen leaves after the storm last night.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: The café was almost empty on Monday morning — there were not any customers at the tables near the window.
content.error_sentence: The café was almost empty on Monday morning — there were not any customers at the tables near the window.
content.corrected_sentence: The café was almost empty on Monday morning — there were hardly any customers at the tables near the window.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: Each team member were given a different task to complete before the end of the working day.
content.error_sentence: Each team member were given a different task to complete before the end of the working day.
content.corrected_sentence: Each team member was given a different task to complete before the end of the working day.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: She had a little confidence in her ability to pass the exam, so she refused to even try signing up for it.
content.error_sentence: She had a little confidence in her ability to pass the exam, so she refused to even try signing up for it.
content.corrected_sentence: She had little confidence in her ability to pass the exam, so she refused to even try signing up for it.

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: There is ___ milk left in the fridge — we need to buy more today.
distractor_rationale: a little means there is some milk, which contradicts the instruction to buy more today
a few is used with countable nouns; milk is uncountable
few is used with countable nouns; milk is uncountable

duplicate: exact duplicate of a prior item

rejected.rejection_reason: duplicate: exact duplicate of a prior item
